def transform_ring(A):
    B=A.copy()
    # interior rows 1..3 inclusive
    for r in range(1,4):
        # shift row left by 1: for cols 1..3 shift; but also include border duplication
        B[r,1:-1]=A[r,2:]
    return B

def letterG_from_ring(h):
    w=h
    A=np.zeros((h,w),dtype=int)
    # outer border
    A[0,:]=1
    A[-1,:]=1
    A[:,0]=1
    A[:,-1]=1
    # step2
    A[1:-1,1]=1
    # step4
    A[1:-1,-1]=0
    # step3
    A[h//2,-2]=1
    return A

```python
def spiral_path(rows,cols):
    r0=0; r1=rows-1; c0=0; c1=cols-1
    order=[]
    while r0<=r1 and c0<=c1:
        for c in range(c0,c1+1):
            order.append((r0,c))
        r0+=1
        for r in range(r0,r1+1):
            order.append((r,c1))
        c1-=1
        if r0<=r1:
            for c in range(c1,c0-1,-1):
                order.append((r1,c))
            r1-=1
        if c0<=c1:
            for r in range(r1,r0-1,-1):
                order.append((r,c0))
            c0+=1
    return order
```

def spiral_path(rows,cols):
    r0=0; r1=rows-1; c0=0; c1=cols-1
    order=[]
    while r0<=r1 and c0<=c1:
        for c in range(c0,c1+1):
            order.append((r0,c))
        r0+=1
        for r in range(r0,r1+1):
            order.append((r,c1))
        c1-=1
        if r0<=r1:
            for c in range(c1,c0-1,-1):
                order.append((r1,c))
            r1-=1
        if c0<=c1:
            for r in range(r1,r0-1,-1):
                order.append((r,c0))
            c0+=1
    return order

```python
def detect_grid_size(img):
    arr=np.array(img)
    mask=((arr!=255).any(axis=2))
    ys,xs=np.where(mask)
    top,bottom=ys.min(), ys.max()
    left,right=xs.min(), xs.max()
    region=arr[top:bottom+1, left:right+1]
    h,w,_=region.shape
    # detect vertical white lines
    is_white_col = (region==255).all(axis=2).mean(axis=0) > 0.95
    white_cols=np.where(is_white_col)[0]
    # cluster continuous white columns into lines
    lines=[]
    start=white_cols[0]
    for i in range(1,len(white_cols)):
        if white_cols[i]!=white_cols[i-1]+1:
            lines.append((start,white_cols[i-1]))
            start=white_cols[i]
    lines.append((start,white_cols[-1]))
    grid_size=len(lines)-1  # for cell separators maybe n+1 lines
    return region, lines, grid_size
```

Rule: Identify the longer bar (the only bar whose length is greater than the thickness of any other coloured structure). Slide every cell of the shorter, perpendicular coloured component horizontally or vertically (i.e. along the axis of the longer bar) until the two bars form a centred “plus”: the point where the two bars would meet lies exactly in the middle of the longer bar. Do not otherwise change either bar’s shape, colour or orientation.

rule_evaluation_reasoning: The Evaluated Rule specifies that the marker square must be at the end of a solid horizontal line and that it must be erased before placing the marker color in a vertical 'plus' formation. This introduces a specific condition (the end-marker square) and a different placement method (vertical 'plus') that diverges from the Ground Truth Rule, which simply states to move a single pixel to the center and extend it perpendicularly. Therefore, the Evaluated Rule does not accurately reflect the transformation described in the Ground Truth Rule.
Rule: For every solid horizontal line (one contiguous colour other than black) that has a single square of a different colour stuck to one of its ends, erase that end-marker square, then place that marker colour at the central cell of the line and also in the cells immediately above and below it, forming a vertical 3-cell ‘plus’ that intersects the horizontal line.

rule_evaluation_reasoning: The Evaluated Rule captures the essence of the Ground Truth Rule by moving a differently colored pixel to the center of the line and extending it vertically, forming a 'plus' shape. While it includes additional specifications about the color and the attachment point, the core transformation functionality remains aligned with the Ground Truth Rule.
Rule: For every horizontal line of one colour that has a single differently-coloured square attached at one of its ends (directly above or below the end cell), move that special square to the middle cell of the horizontal line. Re-colour the original attachment cell with the line’s colour (or background if it is not on the line). Then draw a vertical line of the special colour one cell above and one cell below the new central square, forming a 3-cell vertical stroke and creating a coloured “plus” centred on the horizontal line.

The core transformation remains consistent.
Rule: For every straight monochrome line, locate the single pixel of a different colour that touches one of the line’s end cells orthogonally. Remove that pixel from its original spot, place it on the centre cell of the line (overwriting that line cell) and duplicate it one cell to either side in the direction perpendicular to the line, forming a 3-pixel bar that crosses the original line.

```python
def img_to_grid_general(img, n=7):
    arr=np.array(img)
    h,w,_=arr.shape
    cellh=h//n
    cellw=w//n
    grid=[]
    for r in range(n):
        row=[]
        for c in range(n):
            sub=arr[r*cellh+cellh//2, c*cellw+cellw//2]
            row.append(tuple(sub))
        grid.append(row)
    return grid
```

Rule: There are always two parallel border stripes of the same colour and a single different-coloured cell somewhere inside the grid. Copy (overwrite) the border stripe that is farther away from the single cell into the immediately adjacent inner row/column, thus making that stripe two cells thick while leaving the nearer stripe unchanged.
